coredata.dc:title: An easy approach of preparing strongly luminescent carbon dots and their polymer based composites for enhancing solar cell efficiency
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Jia Jia
Zhong, Zhen Feng
Rong, Min Zhi
Zhou, Xiang
Chen, Xu Dong
Zhang, Ming Qiu
coredata.dc:description: We report a one-step approach for synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) under mild conditions. By using (N-(2-aminoethyl)-3-aminopropyl)tris-(2-ethoxy) silane (KH791) as catalyzer, stabilizing and passivation agent, the resultant CDs are allowed to be self-assembled in solution and exhibit excitation wavelength independent photoluminescence (PL) performance. When the solvent is removed, luminescent CDs filled polysiloxane composite is readily yielded through hydrolytic condensation between KH791 and CDs. According to this habit, CDs can be conveniently utilized by coating the hybrid solution on proper substrates, etc. On the basis of the luminescent down-shifting behavior of the composite with CDs, power conversion efficiency of polymer-fullerene-based bulk heterojunction solar cell is increased by about 12% due to effective light conversion of near ultraviolet and blue-violet portions of sunlight. The simple preparation, easy processing and unique PL properties of CDs and their polymer composite provide the latter with broad applicability.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of P3HT:PCBM based solar cell

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Survey XPS data, (b) C1s, (c) FTIR, and (d) 13C NMR spectra of the CDs collected from dilute solution.

(a) TEM image of CDs. (b) Amplified TEM image of the CDs selected from (a); Inset: HRTEM image of single CD. (c) Size distribution of CDs estimated from (b). (d) AFM topography image of CDs on quartz substrate. (e) AFM 3D image of (d). (f) Height profiles along lines I, II and III in (d). The CDs used for the microscopic measurement were collected from dilute solution.

(a) UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of CDs in aqueous solution (pH=3.4) with and without KH791. The inset photographs of CDs in solution were taken under 365nm irradiation (left: without KH791; right: with KH791). (b) PL spectra of CDs in aqueous solution after different storage times (excitation wavelength=365nm, pH=3.4). (c) PL spectra of CDs in aqueous solution (pH=3.4) with KH791 excited at various wavelengths. (d) Effect of pH value on the PL spectra of CDs with KH791 in aqueous solution.
Schematic illustration of the PL mechanism of self-assembled CDs in solution.
UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of CDs filled polysiloxane composite (∼20μm thick) coated on glass.
Schematic architecture of the BHJ solar cell with LDS layer.
(a) J–V curves of the P3HT:PCBM based BHJ solar cell with and without the LDS layer. (b) Wavelength dependences of EQE of the P3HT:PCBM based solar cell with and without the LDS layer.
Characteristic parameters of P3HT:PCBM based BHJ solar cell with and without LDS layer.

head.ce:title: An easy approach of preparing strongly luminescent carbon dots and their polymer based composites for enhancing solar cell efficiency

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Polymeric Composite and Functional Materials of Ministry of Education, GD HPPC Lab, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510275, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Polymeric Composite and Functional Materials of Ministry of Education
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Lab of Optoelectronic Materials and Technologies, School of Physics and Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510275, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Lab of Optoelectronic Materials and Technologies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a one-step approach for synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) under mild conditions. By using (N-(2-aminoethyl)-3-aminopropyl)tris-(2-ethoxy) silane (KH791) as catalyzer, stabilizing and passivation agent, the resultant CDs are allowed to be self-assembled in solution and exhibit excitation wavelength independent photoluminescence (PL) performance. When the solvent is removed, luminescent CDs filled polysiloxane composite is readily yielded through hydrolytic condensation between KH791 and CDs. According to this habit, CDs can be conveniently utilized by coating the hybrid solution on proper substrates, etc. On the basis of the luminescent down-shifting behavior of the composite with CDs, power conversion efficiency of polymer-fullerene-based bulk heterojunction solar cell is increased by about 12% due to effective light conversion of near ultraviolet and blue-violet portions of sunlight. The simple preparation, easy processing and unique PL properties of CDs and their polymer composite provide the latter with broad applicability.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing interests owing to their excellent optical and conductive properties. Compared with conventional semiconductor nanodots and organic dyes [1], CDs are characterized by low cytotoxicity, chemical inertness, low photobleaching, cost effectiveness, and excellent biocompatibility. As a result, they have broad application prospects in many technologies, including bioimaging [2–4], sensing [5–8], photocatalysis [9–11], fluorescent ink [12–14], light emitting diode and solar cells [15–18].

So far, a variety of methods have been proposed to prepare CDs since the first report of CDs in 2004 [19], such as laser-ablation [2,3], pyrolysis [20,21], combustion [22], hydrothermal process [6,23], template [24,25], electrochemistry [11,26,27], ultrasonic and microwave synthesis [13,28,29]. Relatively complicated procedures and/or strict conditions are required, which hinders large-scale production. With respect to practical application of CDs in solid-state devices, furthermore, there is an open challenge. That is, severe fluorescence quenching used to occur in dried CDs due to aggregation of these tiny particles [13].

In this work, we put forward a one-step low temperature solution approach to settle the aforesaid issue. L-ascorbic acid (L-AA) acts as the carbon source as it is more reactive and acidic than other organic acids, sugar, and amino acids [30]. (N-(2-aminoethyl)-3-aminopropyl)tris-(2-ethoxy) silane (KH791) plays the role of stabilizing and passivation agent, which leads to self-assembling of CDs and excitation wavelength-independent photoluminescence (PL) in solution. What is more, the hybrid comprising silane KH791 and CDs can be further converted into solidified composite through hydrolytic condensation in the course of solvent removal, so that fluorescence quenching is prevented. By taking advantage of the luminescent down-shifting (LDS) ability of the resultant polysiloxane based composite with CDs, power conversion efficiency (PCE) of inverted polymer-fullerene-based bulk heterojunction (BHJ) solar cell made from a blend of poly(3-hexylthiophene):[6,6]-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (P3HT:PCBM) is obviously improved because of the effective light conversion of near ultraviolet and blue-violet portions of sunlight.

KH791 was purchased from Beijing Shenda Fine Chemical Co., Ltd., China. L-AA (99.99%) was supplied by Aldrich. 2-Ketogulonic acid (99%) was supplied by Alfa Aesar. P3HT and PCBM were purchased from Echo Chemical Co., Ltd., Taiwan, China. Ag was purchased from China New Metal Materials Technology Co., Ltd., China. ZnO was prepared according to the literature [31]. MoO3 was purchased from Beijing Aglaia Technology Development Co., Ltd., China. Other chemicals were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory, China. Ultra-purified water was used throughout all experiments.

Typically, 2.64g L-AA was dissolved in 300ml mixture solution of ethanol and water (1:1, v/v). Then, 7.92g KH791 was added and the reaction proceeded at 92°C for 12h. The resultant light yellow solution was filtered through 0.2μm polytetrafluoroethylene membrane to remove large particles or other impurities. The as-obtained product was directly used for making LDS layer of solar cell (see the next section), while the product for various characterizations was neutralized through dialysis (membrane molecular weight cutoff ∼1000) against a large volume of ultra-purified water. The reference sample was prepared under the same condition but KH791 was excluded. 0.1mol/L NaOH and 0.1mol/L HCl were used to adjust pH value of the solution containing CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of P3HT:PCBM based solar cell
ce:section.ce:para: The inverted BHJ solar cell is based on a blend of P3HT and PCBM. The glass substrate covered by transparent electrode of indium tin oxide (ITO) with sheet resistance of 50Ω/square was ultrasonically cleaned in acetone, alcohol, and ultra-purified water for 10min, respectively, and then treated by O2 plasma for 10min. Afterwards, zinc oxide (ZnO, ∼30nm thick) was spin coated on the substrate. Having been dried at 150°C for 10min, a film (∼50nm thick) of the blend of P3HT:PCBM with a weight ratio of 1:1 in chlorobenzene (10mg/mL) was further spin-coated on ZnO. This active layer was covered by evaporated MoO3 (∼10nm thick) and Ag electrode (∼100nm thick) in 6×104 Pa vacuum. For examining the effect of the composite with CDs, the solution containing CDs without purification was spin-coated on the other side of the glass (∼20μm thick), and dried at 70°C in atmosphere overnight.

The synthesis of CDs is rather simple here. KH791 was added to the solution of L-AA, ethanol and water, and then the reaction proceeded at 92°C for 12h, giving birth to silane modified CDs. Having been dried at 70°C, KH791 was polymerized and the composite consisting of CDs and polysiloxane was yielded [21].

On the basis of a series of tracking experiments we find out that the mechanism involved in formation of CDs consists of the steps of hydrolysis, polymerization and carbonization. Dry L-AA possesses strong reducibility, but it would be readily oxidized in water, especially in the presence of alkali. At the beginning of the reaction, L-AA is oxidized into dehydroascorbate, and then the unstable dehydroascorbate undergoes irreversible ring cleavage, producing 2-ketogulonic or 2,3-diketogulonic acid in aqueous solution [32]. This is the first step called “hydrolysis”, during which water is the prerequisite condition (Fig. S1). The second step “polymerization” is induced by intermolecular dehydration or aldol condensation between two 2-ketogulonic (or between 2-ketogulonic and L-AA) [33,34]. The yellow or orange color and the increased viscosity of the resultant solution imply that aromatic compounds and oligosaccharides are generated [35], which is denoted by polymer-like CDs (p-CDs). Afterwards, carbogenic CDs are produced by the third step “carbonization” of p-CDs through intramolecular dehydration or keto-enol tautomerism [33,34]. Meanwhile, reaction temperature is crucial to the transformation from p-CDs to CDs (Fig. S2). In case of lower reaction temperature, the product has to stay at p-CDs. Only when the temperature is higher than 80°C, CDs can be obtained. In addition, excess water is adverse to intramolecular dehydration, which would prevent p-CDs from being transformed into CDs (Fig. S1). KH791 not only serves as catalyzer due to its alkaline that accelerates the hydrolysis and oxidation of L-AA, but also passivation agent that greatly enhances PL property of the CDs (Fig. S3) [3,20]. In addition, the polysiloxane formed at the surface of CDs through hydrolytic condensation of silicon–oxygen linkage during drying prevents the CDs from aggregation. The PL property of CDs is also dependent on reaction time (Fig. S4 and Table S1).

To verify the above mechanism, commercial 2-ketogulonic is employed as the raw reactant. CDs with similar PL property can be prepared under the same reaction condition with L-AA (Fig. S5). It is evident that hydrolysis of L-AA is necessary for the formation of CDs. In addition, the p-CDs (prepared under 70°C) and CDs (prepared under 90°C) were also characterized by AFM using tapping and peak-force modes (Fig. S6). As shown in Fig. S6a, the p-CDs are wormlike and arborization, exhibiting typical morphology of polymer chains aggregates. In contrast, the CDs are uniform granules smaller than p-CDs (Fig. S6b). The AFM deformation images of p-CDs (Fig. S6c) and CDs (Fig. S6d) indicate that p-CDs have larger deformation than CDs under the same applied force. The higher degree of carbonization of CDs than p-CDs should account for the difference.

The surface functional groups of the produced CDs were characterized by XPS XPS, FTIR and 13C NMR spectra (Fig. 1 ). Clearly, C, O, Si and N appear on the surface of CDs in accordance with the designed recipe (Fig. 1a). Moreover, deconvolution of the C1s spectrum (Fig. 1b) reveals the presence of three types of carbon bonds: sp2 CC (284.81ev), C–O (286.29ev) and CO (288.14ev) [12]. They represent the existence of carboxylic and hydroxyl groups as well as the conjugated structures of CC and CO. Fig. 1c illustrates that the broad peak centered at 3390cm− 1 is attributed to stretching vibration of O–H and N–H. Along with the stretching of C–OH at 1200cm− 1, it can be concluded that there are extensive hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs. On the other hand, the sharp peak assigned to amide linkage (–CONH–) found at 1690cm− 1 indicates amidation between KH791 and CDs [36]. The stretching mode of CO group at 1763cm−1 and the out-plane deformation vibration of carboxylic acid dimmer (OH⋯O) at 916cm− 1 manifest the contribution of carboxylic groups. Meanwhile, the peaks at 1109 and 1049cm− 1 are attributed to stretching of Si–O–Si, and the peaks at 2938 and 2878cm− 1 originate from stretching of C–H. In the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. 1d), the signals in the range of 0–100ppm correspond to aliphatic (sp3) carbon atoms, while those from 100 to 175ppm correspond to sp2 carbon atoms [14,30].

The interaction between hydroxyl and carbonyl on the surface of CDs would lead to hydrogen bonding, so that self-assembling of CDs may emerge. The observation results in terms of TEM and AFM prove this is the case. It is seen that the prepared CDs mainly appear in the form of assembled structure (Fig. 2 a), consisting of dozens of single CDs (Fig. 2b and c). No aggregated CDs are perceived (Figs. 2b and S7). The AFM images (Figs. 2d–f, S8 and S9) also reflect the self-assembling effect from another angle. As shown by the height profiles in Fig. 2f, the assembled structures are comprised of many CDs with slightly different heights. Besides, since the heights of assembled structures resemble those of single CDs, the self-assembled CDs must be arranged as monolayer.

Careful analysis of Fig. 2 allows quantification of the CDs. Statistics on the basis of TEM images like Fig. 2b indicates that size of the single CDs ranges between 3 and 4nm, which well agrees with the measurement of AFM images (Fig. 2f). Furthermore, crystalline structure of the as-prepared CDs has been identified (refer to the inset of Fig. 2b). The lattice planes with interplanar spacing of 0.204 and 0.176nm are close to (102) and (004) facets of sp2 graphitic carbon, implying that the CDs are structured like crystalline graphite carbon [37].

CDs are known for their PL, which must be affected by the self-assembled microstructure discussed above. Firstly, UV–vis spectrum of aqueous solution of the CDs is studied (Fig. 3 a). A significant absorption peak is detected at the wavelength of about 360nm, which forms sharp contrast to L-AA and KH791, as the latter two have no absorption in this wavelength region (Fig. S10). Therefore, the absorption peak in Fig. 3a should be attributed to n–π* transition of multi-conjugate CC of the CDs [38]. It is interesting to find that, under 365nm excitation, the solution with CDs gives peak fluorescence emission at 547nm with a fluorescent quantum yield of 3.8% (using quinine sulfate as a reference). When KH791 is present, the emission intensity is greatly enhanced and the fluorescent quantum yield is increased to 8.6%, which is equivalent to or higher than the values of CDs documented in the literature [9,12,30]. Such intensification can be directly seen by visual inspection. As shown by the inset of Fig. 3a, luminance of the solution with both CDs and KH791 is much stronger than that without KH791. In the meantime, average fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs-containing aqueous solution without and with KH791 are determined to be 2.1 and 3.0ns [12], respectively (Figs. S11 and S12). The longer fluorescence lifetime indicates more stable excited-state, which is conductive to higher quantum yield [14]. In fact, the solution with CDs we prepared exhibits extra-high stability. There is nearly no fluorescence decay during three-month ambient storage (Fig. 3b) and after exposure to 500W ultraviolet light for 120min (Fig. S13).

Generally, PL wavelength of either CDs or graphene quantum dots is a function of excitation wavelength [26,39]. For the current solution with CDs, however, the wavelength of peak emission keeps constant (547nm) when excitation wavelength varies from 340 to 450nm (Fig. 3c). Similarly, with a rise in pH value of the solution with CDs from 3.4 to 9.8, the excitation peaks red shift from 365 to 425nm, while the emission peak positions remain nearly unchanged and the PL intensity attains to the strongest value at pH=6.0 (Fig. 3d). In principle, fluorescence emissions in CDs are attributed to radiative recombinations of the photoinduced electrons and holes that are confined to the carbon nanoparticle surface. CDs serve as not only electron acceptors but also election donors [39–41]. Under acidic condition, the positive charges in solution increase trapping of excited-state energy and impede electron–hole radiative recombination on the surface of CDs, which induces blue shift of excitation peaks and decreases the PL intensity. Under alkaline condition, the excessive negative charges occupy the holes formed on the surface of CDs, leading to gradually reduced PL intensity with increasing alkality.

The excitation wavelength independent PL performance might result from the uniform size of CDs [42], but the argument is challenged by the fact that after removing the solvent in the solution containing CDs and KH791, PL wavelength of the polysiloxane based composite with CDs becomes excitation wavelength dependent (Fig. S14). With increasing excitation wavelength from 350 to 450nm, the emission peaks range from 457 to 528nm. It means that different mechanism is involved. We believe that both defect state emission and intrinsic state emission contribute to the fluorescence. Luminescence of the CDs in solution mainly stems from defect state emission. In fact, various defects of the CDs are responsible for absorptions and emissions, respectively [40]. KH791 acts as a passivation agent and stabilizes the energy trapping sites on the carbon nanoparticle surface, so that the luminance intensity of CDs is increased accordingly [41]. In the solution with CDs, each self-assembled structure consisting of single CDs plays the role of PL unit (Fig. 4 ). The PL performance of these units is the result of interaction through electron transfer or cross-recombination among the CDs in the self-assembled structures. Because the combined action of the CDs in the self-assembled structures, rather than single CDs, determines luminescence, PL wavelength of the solution containing CDs does not change with excitation wavelength regardless of whether the pH value is 3.4 or 6.0 (Fig. S15).

The wavelength of peak emission of CDs is closely related to their size. The larger the CDs, the longer wavelength of peak emission [11]. Since the self-assembled structures are much bigger than single CDs, the wavelength of peak emission of the solution with CDs is as long as 547nm, the longest value ever known to the authors. It in turn evidences the above deduction that the self-assembled structures but not single CDs are responsible for PL. When the solution is converted to composite solid, the surrounding insulating polysiloxane prevents either electron transfer or cross-recombination among the CDs. As a result, the PL performance is controlled by the single CDs. The isolated sp2 nano-domains dispersed within the sp3 matrix would function as PL centers with properties governed by π states of sp2 sites [43]. In this context, different sp2 nano-domains can be excited at different wavelengths, which leads to different emission peaks. That is, the excitation wavelength independent PL performance is no longer observed (Fig. S14).

An important information revealed by Fig. S14 lies in the fact that there is no fluorescence quenching that used to occur in dried CDs. Photo properties of the CDs filled polysiloxane composite can thus be used conveniently (Fig. S16). Figs. S14 and 5 further indicate that the composite exhibits strong absorption of the light spanning 320–450nm, and emitted light from 400 to 650nm. Its absolute quantum yield reaches to 32% [21]. The LDS behavior reminds us of one of the shortcomings of P3HT:PCBM based BHJ solar cell. It harvests sunlight from 480 to 650nm, only covering a part of visible light (380–780nm) that contains the peak of the Sun’s irradiance output. The rest portions (380–480nm and 650–780nm) are not effectively utilized. Since the CDs filled polysiloxane composite is able to emit light from 400 to 650nm under the excitation from 320 to 450nm, its wavelength ranges of both PL and excitation well fit the harvesting spectrum (480–650nm) and unused light spectrum (380–480nm) of the aforesaid type of BHJ solar cell. When the composite stands between the sun and the solar cell, the sunlight utilization efficiency of the latter should be improved accordingly.

On the basis of this consideration, syrup of CDs was spin coated on the cover glass of an inverted P3HT:PCBM based BHJ solar cell to form a LDS layer (Fig. 6 ), which doesn’t take part in either charge generation or carriers transport. Fig. 7 a shows the J–V curves of the solar cells without and with the LDS layer. From the plots in Fig. 7a, characteristic parameters of the cells are calculated and listed in Table 1 . For the reference device, a short circuit current density (J sc) of around 7.65mA/cm2 is accompanied by an open circuit voltage (V oc) of around 0.60V, a fill factor (FF) of 0.62 and a PCE of 2.85%. When the device is coupled with a piece of bare cover glass, its J sc, V oc, FF and PCE are 7.63mA/cm2, 0.60V, 0.62% and 2.84%, respectively, which are mostly similar to the values of the reference cell except for small losses due to appearance of the glass. After incorporation of the LDS layer, obviously improved performance is measured. J sc increases from 7.65 to 8.40mA/cm2, while V oc and FF are nearly unchanged, resulting in a total PCE of 3.18%, which is increased by a factor of about 12% in comparison to the reference device.

To elucidate the reason for the increase in J sc and PCE, measurements of EQE were carried out (Fig. 7b). The EQE curves of the devices without the LDS layer overlap, but that of the cell with the LDS layer is intensified in the wavelength range between 350 and 450nm. It proves that the above expectation about increasing utilization efficiency of the light from 380 to 480nm through the LDS function of the composite with CDs has come true. More incident sunlight has been converted to the light that the BHJ solar cell is able to respond. The gain due to LDS is large enough to increase EQE and PCE [44]. Above 450nm, the EQE values of all the devices are the same as it exceeds the upper limit of the excitation wavelength of the LDS layer (Fig. 5). In comparison with the measures to enhance efficiency of BHJ solar cells developed by other laboratories [45–51], the present one has the advantages like stability under sunlight, non-toxicity, higher quantum efficiency, and easy handling.

We have developed a simple and effective method to prepare CDs and their polymer based composite. The key issue lies in the addition of silane KH791 into the reaction system, which acts as not only passivation agent but also polymerizable monomer. It helps to enhance PL intensity of CDs, induce self-assembling of CDs in solution, and prevent CDs from agglomeration in solidified composite. Accordingly, the composite with CDs luminesces and exhibits luminescent down-shifting habit. Because the emission wavelength range of the composite coincides with the response curve of P3HT:PCBM based BHJ solar cell, power conversion efficiency of the latter is raised by about 12% through coating a layer of the composite on the cover glass as a result of enhanced absorption of near ultraviolet and blue-violet portions of sunlight. It is believed that the present work opens up an avenue towards large scale application of CDs in solid-state devices such as solar cells and light emitting diodes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the financial support by the Science and Technology Program of Guangdong Province (Grant: 2010A011300004), Education Department of Guangdong (Grant: cxzd1001), and the project of key technological breakthrough for emerging industries of strategic importance (Grants: 2011A091102001 and 2011A091102003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2013.12.092.

ce:e-component.ce:label: Supplementary Figs. S1–S16 and Table S1 1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary material contains Figs. S1–S16 and Table S1.
